frameworks.name: E/I balance
frameworks.one_line: GABAergic inhibition and NMDA-mediated glutamatergic excitation jointly shape cortical population dynamics.

frameworks.name: cortical hierarchy
frameworks.one_line: Intrinsic dynamics organized along the anatomical hierarchy of cortical areas.

frameworks.name: receptor-density alignment
frameworks.one_line: Spatial maps of dynamic alterations compared with GABA-A receptor density maps and pharmacological challenge maps.

core_question: How do distributed GABAergic and glutamatergic synaptic alterations in early-stage psychosis reshape intrinsic cortical population dynamics, and do these patterns relate to symptom dimensions?
key_assumptions: MEG source imaging reflects intrinsic neural population dynamics across cortex.
Pharmacological GABA-A / NMDA challenges in healthy participants index receptor-specific dynamic signatures.
Cortex-wide dynamic features are organized by anatomical hierarchy.
manipulations: Clinical group (first-episode psychosis vs clinical high risk vs healthy).
Pharmacological challenge in healthy participants (GABA-A vs NMDA).
Multiple features of intrinsic neural population dynamics across the cortex.
key_findings: Cortex-wide patterns of dynamic features were highly reproducible and tracked the anatomical hierarchy.
Similar alteration patterns appeared in first-episode psychosis and clinical high-risk individuals.
Psychosis alteration maps resembled GABA-A receptor density maps and pharmacological GABA-A / NMDA effect maps.
Individual pattern similarity to GABA-A effects correlated with positive symptoms; similarity to NMDA effects correlated with negative symptoms.
interpretations: Heterogeneous psychosis symptoms map onto dissociable GABAergic vs glutamatergic alterations of cortical dynamics.
Receptor-aligned dynamic maps provide a bridge from distributed synaptic pathology to symptom dimensions.
